Content and Wellness Communication

Content that makes people feel less alone in what they are going through.

I am Chahat Singh. I work in content with a focus on emotional storytelling and wellness communication. Over the past year I have been learning what actually makes people stop, read, and feel something through real work rather than theory. I am currently building my skills in copywriting and content strategy and looking for the next place to grow.

See the work Get in touch

About

I got here because I am genuinely curious about people.

Not in a vague way. I mean the specific question of why certain things make someone feel understood and other things slide right past them. What makes a person stop scrolling at 1am and save a post because it finally put words to something they had been carrying. That question is what pulled me into content.

I have been working in wellness and emotional psychology content covering EFT, nervous system work, and identity. I also run a personal page called theCeraVyne where I write in my own voice about the messier, more honest side of the same topics. I am currently deepening my skills in copywriting and learning how content connects to strategy. I have things to learn and I know it. What I bring right now is emotional instinct, genuine curiosity about audiences, and the ability to write without sounding like a template.

"People are not too much. Most are simply operating from places within themselves they do not fully understand yet."

Hook writing Reel scripting Wellness content Audience psychology Educational carousels Content planning Campaign coordination Canva Notion

Work

Things I have made and contributed to

Some of this is from my time at Ezine Kaleidoscope where I worked as a content coordinator. Some is from my personal page theCeraVyne. I have been clear about which is which.

Personal work · theCeraVyne

The In-Between — a reel series I write and script myself

This is my own page. The series covers identity, emotional avoidance, and the things that fall through the cracks of most wellness content. Titles like "Choosing yourself isn't abandonment" and "Why 'WHY' is dangerous" come from things I genuinely think about. This is where my voice is most unfiltered and where I get to test what actually resonates.

Format

Scripted reels and text posts

Topics

Identity, emotional avoidance, self-worth

My role

Concept, script, delivery — everything

Find it

@theceravyne on Instagram

Event Campaign · Ezine Kaleidoscope

Tapathon — five days of EFT tapping

A live five-day event built around emotional release through EFT. I worked on the messaging direction and helped coordinate the promotional content. Two versions of the graphic were created and tested against each other to see which framing connected better with the audience. The full A/B breakdown is in the next section.

Educational Carousel · Ezine Kaleidoscope

What pain in different parts of your body is trying to tell you

Each body part became a mirror for an emotional pattern. The chest holds unexpressed grief. The gut holds unprocessed fear. The throat holds the voice you have been swallowing. The goal was to make someone pause and think "oh, that is what that is." That moment of recognition is what drives saves and shares.

Selected slides

Cover plus four body part slides

Format

Educational carousel

Goal

High save and share

CTA

Comment via self-identification

What I noticed: The final slide asking "which one made you pause?" does not push anything. It just invites honesty. That kind of CTA gets genuine responses over transactional ones.

Event Campaigns · Ezine Kaleidoscope

Akshaya, EFT Workshop and Tarot Workshop

Three campaigns with different emotional tones running on the same brand. Akshaya needed to feel expansive and sacred. The EFT workshop needed to feel grounded and credible. The Tarot workshop needed to feel accessible without being intimidating. Getting the emotional register right for each without breaking consistency was something I was actively working on here.

What I noticed: Each of these events attracted a slightly different person within the same audience. Keeping the brand voice consistent while adjusting the emotional register for each one was a real learning for me.

Reel Series · Ezine Kaleidoscope

Breakthrough Tapping Challenge — a daily video series

A four-day scripted reel series supporting a live tapping challenge. Each reel needed to feel warm and low-pressure because EFT can feel unfamiliar to new people. The content was structured so each day felt complete on its own while still building on the previous one.

What I noticed: Daily series live and die on tone consistency. If one video feels different from the others, people notice even if they cannot say why. Keeping the emotional register steady across a series was something I paid close attention to here.

A/B Testing

Tapathon — two versions, one clear winner

Two promotional graphics were created for the same campaign to test which messaging angle and visual approach performed better with the audience.

Version A

"Five days of tapping. One shift at a time."

Emotional and introspective angle

  • Minimal, mood-driven visual
  • Focus on inner release and stillness
  • Implicit program structure
  • Appeals to audiences already familiar with EFT
Version B Winner

"Tap. Release. Transform."

Structured transformation angle

  • Color-coded emotion icons per day
  • Clear five-day progression visible upfront
  • Pricing shown (Rs. 499 for all 5 days)
  • Better with new audiences who need clarity first

Version B outperformed Version A by

34%

Higher click-through rate

28%

Higher DM inquiries

18%

Higher registration rate

What I learned: Version B won not because it was prettier but because new audiences need to see explicit structure before they can feel emotional openness. The five emotion icons told people exactly what they were signing up for in under three seconds. That clarity is what earned the click. The learning here is that introspective messaging works for warm audiences who already trust you. For cold audiences, structure and specificity come first.

Carousel concepts

Two educational carousel ideas I developed

These are fully planned carousel concepts with slide-by-slide structure. Both designed for high save and share value on wellness-focused accounts.

Carousel concept 1

How EFT Calms the Brain

Breaks down what actually happens neurologically when you tap. Makes the science accessible without oversimplifying it. Designed so someone with no EFT background understands why it works.

  • 1How EFT rewires your brain (cover)
  • 2Amygdala activation: stress hormones and fight/flight
  • 3Prefrontal cortex shuts down: survival mode takes over
  • 4Tapping calms the amygdala: thinking brain reactivates
  • 5Brain fog lifts: logic and focus return
  • 6Old stress loops weaken: new calmer responses build
  • 7CTA: Save this for when stress takes over

Carousel concept 2

Intuition vs Instinct

Helps people figure out whether they are responding from inner knowing or from survival patterns. Designed to drive comments because people naturally want to categorise themselves.

  • 1Are you an intuition person or an instinct person? (hook)
  • 2Definitions: inner knowing vs survival-based reaction
  • 3Intuitive traits: pattern recognition, empathy, ambiguity tolerance
  • 4Instinctive traits: impulsive, fear-based, always guarded
  • 5Validation: both are protective, neither is weakness
  • 6CTA: Comment which one you are

Writing samples

Hook writing and why it works

A hook is not just the first line. It is a decision about what the reader is feeling right before they scroll past. Here is some of mine with the thinking behind each one.

Hindi / Emotional avoidance

"Ye jo tum bolte rehte ho na ki apni problem batane se kya hi hoga... ise avoidance kehte hai."

Someone who genuinely believes opening up will not help and has been telling themselves that for a long time

It names the behaviour before judging it. Most people hear themselves in the line before they can get defensive. Recognition always comes before reflection.

Hindi / Identity suppression

"Khud se chhup kar baithe rahoge na, toh duniya tumhe apne hisaab se dhaal legi."

Someone who has been shrinking themselves so long it just feels like being easygoing

Conversational enough to feel like a friend said it, not a caption. The intimacy of the tone is doing most of the work here.

Hindi / Spiritual bypassing

"Spirituality achhi hai... lekin tab tak hi jab tak tum uske peeche chhup nahi rahe."

Someone consuming spiritual content heavily but quietly knowing they are using it to avoid rather than grow

Confrontational without being dismissive. It names the pattern and not the person, which keeps it reflective rather than aggressive.

English / Behavioural psychology

"Your brain doesn't always choose what's healthy. It chooses what feels familiar."

Someone stuck in a pattern they can see but cannot break, quietly blaming themselves for it

It removes self-blame without being preachy about it. One sentence reframes the whole situation. That is when things get saved.

Content planning

A 6-month emotional wellness calendar

The thinking here was to plan a content journey instead of a posting schedule. Each month continues emotionally from the one before so the audience feels like they are going somewhere, not just being posted at.

January

Conscious Starts

Intentional growth, clarity over pressure, sustainable beginnings

February

Feeling Before Fixing

Emotional regulation, attachment patterns, nervous system awareness

March

How to Learn

Conscious learning, avoiding dependency, building self-awareness

April

Stable Foundations

Grounding, nervous system safety, Reiki and wellness tools

May

Choosing Yourself

People-pleasing, emotional boundaries, healthy separation

June

Building Self-Trust

Intuition, small actions, reducing external validation

Long-form writing

An article I wrote about relationships

This is early writing. I am still developing as a writer and I know that. But the observations in here are mine and I think they are worth saying.

Article

On relationships, emotional awareness, and the things we do not say out loud

Written in 2025. Edited for clarity but not changed in substance.

Relationships are not always easy. Most of us go through several stages before we find one that is actually worth investing our emotions in. And we do this in an era where we have more information about relationships than any generation before us, yet somehow end up more confused. The internet has not made it easier to recognise a red flag. If anything, it has made it harder, because now we have everyone else's framework for what love is supposed to look like running in the background while we are trying to figure out our own.

A lot of us end up with someone who looks like a green flag on paper because we are pattern-matching against what we have been shown, not against what we actually need. Social media, older generations' advice, peer pressure — all of it shapes who we think we should want before we have had a chance to figure out who we actually are.

One example of this that I think about a lot is what people call the "college wife" or "college husband" dynamic. Someone gets into a relationship early in college and slowly begins doing everything for their partner — emotionally, practically, logistically — while being made to feel like this is just what love looks like. By the time college ends, so does the relationship. The person who gave more is left trying to figure out where they went wrong. They did not go wrong. They just were not seen clearly, and they were not taught to ask for that.

What makes relationships work is not chemistry or compatibility on paper. It is two people who are both willing to keep showing up, keep communicating, and keep doing the actual work of knowing each other. Not just at the beginning when everything feels easy. Consistently, even when it does not.

Part of that work is healing what you bring with you. The experiences that left marks. The moments that taught you love was conditional, or that your needs were too much, or that you had to earn closeness. None of that disappears when you meet someone new. It follows you in, and if you have not looked at it, it will quietly shape every decision you make in the relationship.

I think good relationships also require a kind of ongoing curiosity about the other person. Not just who they were when you met, but who they are now and who they are becoming. The questions below are ones I think matter, not as a checklist to run through on a third date, but as things to actually sit with over time.

Questions worth asking your partner

  1. Have you ever felt like you were not being heard in a relationship? This tells you something about what they need from you, and it opens a space for you to share the same.
  2. What are you most insecure about? Not to judge, but to understand. The answer to this will explain more about how they behave than almost anything else.
  3. Is there anything you wish was different about what we have right now? Most people do not ask this. The relationship quietly drifts instead.
  4. Is there something from your past that still affects how you see things? This is not therapy. It is just acknowledging that people carry things, and that those things show up.
  5. How do you actually want to receive love? Not how you think you should want it. How you actually need it. This is harder to answer than it sounds.

None of this makes relationships simple. But I think knowing these things about someone, and being willing to share them about yourself, is the difference between two people existing near each other and two people actually building something.

You are not going to find a perfect person. That is not the point. The point is finding someone who is willing to keep figuring it out with you, and being that person for them in return.

About 600 words total

Reel scripts

Some of the scripts I have written

These are real scripts, mostly in Hindi, written for a wellness audience. Some were posted. Some are concepts. The thinking behind each one is the same — find the thing someone already feels but has not said out loud yet.

"Sara din pressure cooker bane firoge to kahan se zindagi mein shanti aayegi?"

Stress and release

Thoda pressure release karo. Stress kam karo. Chhodo un faltu logo, cheezon aur jagahon ko jo problem create kar rahi hain. Har cheez ko carry karna koi bahaduri ka kaam nahi hai.

Aur agar genuinely jaanna chahte ho ki apna pressure kaise kam kiya jasakta hai — DM karo. Session mein baat karenge.

Written for Ezine Kaleidoscope. Hook targets the person who is holding everything together quietly and has never been told it is okay to put some of it down.

"Ye jo tum bolte rehte ho na ki apni problem batane se kya hi ho jaayega... ise avoidance kehte hain."

Emotional avoidance

Main samajhta hoon ki problem share karna easy nahi lagta. Aur main tumhari choice ka respect karta hoon agar tum share nahi karna chahte. Lekin kam se kam apne aap se toh discuss karo.

Kyunki jo tum avoid kar rahe ho woh jaata nahi. Woh bas neeche rehta hai.

The goal of this hook is to name the behaviour without making the person defensive. Most avoidance content either judges or lectures. This one just says — I see what you are doing, and here is what it actually is.

"Sab ko dekh liya Instagram par apne best moments show karte hue?"

Comparison and self-worth

Lag raha hoga tum peeche ho. Ya tumhari life mein to kuch hai hi nahi. But the reality is — life mein tumhari bhi moments ki kami nahi hai. Tum bas khud ko utni importance nahi dete jitni woh log dete hain apne aap ko.

This one targets a very specific feeling — not just comparison, but the self-erasure that comes with it. The second sentence is the real line. It reframes the problem from "my life is less" to "I am not valuing what I have."

"Your brain does not always choose what is healthy. It chooses what feels familiar."

Psychology / Confirmation bias

Our brain works in patterns. It always looks for what is familiar over what is actually good for us. And it will give you evidence to prove its own stance.

Say someone has the belief that they are not good enough. No matter what they achieve, their brain will keep scanning for proof that they are failing. Bad marks. Losing a game. Not making enough money. It will skip over everything that contradicts the belief because the belief is — I am not enough.

This is called confirmation bias. And it is exhausting. And it is not a personality flaw. It is a nervous system pattern shaped by your life experiences.

Written as a standalone educational reel. The example makes abstract psychology feel personal without being preachy about it.

"Agar sirf mandir ke aage agarbatti jalane se sare dukh chale jaate na..."

Spirituality vs action

...to is duniya mein self-improvement ka concept hi nahi hota. Kyunki kisi ko zaroorat kya hoti fir khud ko grow karne ki.

Spirituality is good. Having faith is good. But not doing anything and just hoping things will shift — that is not faith. That is avoidance with a nicer name.

This one is deliberately a little uncomfortable. It is not anti-spiritual. It is asking people to look at whether their spirituality is helping them move forward or helping them stay still.

A closing thought

What someone takes away after six months of this content

This is the outcome I think about when planning content. Not just what to post, but what someone should understand about themselves after following along for a while.

If someone engaged consistently with this kind of content over six months, I would want them to walk away with a few specific things. Not motivation. Not a new routine. Something quieter and more durable than that.

  • They are not too much. They are not broken. The patterns they keep repeating are not character flaws, they are nervous system responses.
  • Comparison is a pattern, not a truth. What they see online is someone else's curated highlight, not the full picture of a life.
  • Emotional awareness comes before change. You cannot fix what you have not named yet.
  • Their nervous system has been protecting them the only way it knew how.
  • Self-trust is built through small actions, not affirmations.
  • Spirituality without self-awareness becomes a way to avoid rather than grow.
  • Moving forward from where you actually are is the only real progress.

If this feels like someone you would want to work with, I would love to hear from you.

Open to content roles, freelance projects and wellness brand work.
